The Δεκάπολις also recalls Mark 5 — same region where the Gerasene was healed and proclaimed Jesus. Jesus may be returning to fertile soil prepared by the Gerasene's witness.

greek: καὶ φέρουσιν αὐτῷ κωφὸν καὶ μογιλάλον,καὶ παρακαλοῦσιν αὐτὸν ἵνα ἐπιθῇ αὐτῷ τὴν χεῖρα.
bsb_english: Some people brought to Him a man who was deaf and hardly able to speak, and they begged Jesus to place His hand on him.

translation.thai: มีคนพาชายคนหนึ่งซึ่งเป็นคนหูหนวกและพูดติดขัด มาเฝ้าพระองค์ และทูลอ้อนวอนพระองค์ให้ทรงวางพระหัตถ์บนคนผู้นั้น

key_decisions.thai: มีคนพา ... มาเฝ้าพระองค์
key_decisions.rationale: Per uW writing-pronouns: 'they' is impersonal — 'people' in general. Historic present φέρουσιν heightens narrative immediacy. Thai 'มีคนพา ... มาเฝ้า' (there-is-people-bring ... come-attend-on royal) renders with the impersonal-existential construction. 'เฝ้า' (attend on) is the formal verb for approaching a high person — fitting royal Jesus.

key_decisions.greek: κωφὸν καὶ μογιλάλον (HAPAX μογιλάλος + KEY OT ECHO)
key_decisions.thai: คนหูหนวกและพูดติดขัด
key_decisions.rationale: HAPAX (only here in NT, but a CRUCIAL OT-LXX echo): μογιλάλος = literally 'speaking-with-difficulty' (μόγις 'with difficulty' + λάλος 'talkative/speaking'). LXX uses μογιλάλος in Isa 35:6 LXX: 'τότε ἁλεῖται ὡς ἔλαφος ὁ χωλός, καὶ τρανὴ ἔσται γλῶσσα μογιλάλων' ('then the lame will leap like a deer and the tongue of the dumb/mute will speak clearly') — a key Messianic prophecy of restoration. By choosing μογιλάλος (rather than the more common ἄλαλος or κωφός standalone), Mark deliberately echoes Isa 35:6 LXX. Combined with κωφός (deaf, also in Isa 35:5 'ears of the deaf opened'), this is a deliberate Messianic-fulfillment marker — Jesus enacts Isa 35:5–6. Thai 'หูหนวกและพูดติดขัด' (ear-deaf and speak-stuck/halting) renders both — 'พูดติดขัด' (speech-impeded) captures μογιλάλος's halting-speech sense. SEE NOTES on the OT echo.

key_decisions.thai: ทรงวางพระหัตถ์บนคนผู้นั้น
key_decisions.rationale: Per uW figs-explicit: laying-on of hand was the customary gesture of healing-blessing (cf. Mark 5:23 Jairus). Royal 'ทรงวาง' + 'พระหัตถ์' (royal-hand) per RULES.md §3. The supplicants' request specifies the standard Jesus-healing technique — they had heard of his methods. This makes Jesus' subsequent unusual technique (vv.33–34) the more noteworthy.

translation.notes: HAPAX μογιλάλος + MESSIANIC OT ECHO (Isa 35:5–6 LXX): 'Then the eyes of the blind shall be opened and the ears of the deaf unstopped; then shall the lame man leap like a deer and the tongue of the dumb (μογιλάλων) shall sing for joy.' Mark's word-choice is not casual — μογιλάλος is rare in all of Greek literature, and by using it (rather than ἄλαλος 'mute' or κωφός 'deaf' alone), Mark signals to readers familiar with LXX that Isa 35 is being fulfilled. This frames the healing as a Messianic-sign episode, complementing the chapter's other Messianic markers (the πάντα καλῶς πεποίηκεν of v.37, echoing Gen 1's good-creation). Add to nt_ot_citations.json. The healing follows the standard Markan exorcism-sequence (request → unusual technique → command → confirmation), but the OT-echo lifts it from individual healing into Messianic-fulfillment.

greek: καὶ ἀπολαβόμενος αὐτὸν ἀπὸ τοῦ ὄχλου κατ’ἰδίαν ἔβαλεν τοὺς δακτύλους αὐτοῦ εἰς τὰ ὦτα αὐτοῦ καὶ πτύσας ἥψατο τῆς γλώσσης αὐτοῦ,
bsb_english: So Jesus took him aside privately, away from the crowd, and put His fingers into the man's ears. Then He spit and touched the man's tongue.

translation.thai: พระองค์ทรงพาชายนั้นแยกออกจากฝูงชนไปอยู่ตามลำพัง ทรงเอานิ้วพระหัตถ์ยอนเข้าไปในหูของเขา ทรงถ่มน้ำลายและแตะลิ้นของเขา

key_decisions.greek: ἀπολαβόμενος αὐτὸν ἀπὸ τοῦ ὄχλου κατ’ ἰδίαν
key_decisions.thai: ทรงพาชายนั้นแยกออกจากฝูงชนไปอยู่ตามลำพัง
key_decisions.rationale: ἀπολαμβάνω + middle = take-aside; κατ’ ἰδίαν = privately, alone. Royal 'ทรงพา' (royal-bring/lead). Thai 'แยกออกจาก ... ไปอยู่ตามลำพัง' (separate-out-from ... go-stay-by-self) preserves the deliberate separation. Pattern matches Mark 5:40 (Jesus dismissing the crowd before raising Jairus' daughter) — the privacy-motif: significant healings often happen away from spectacle (a Markan messianic-secret tendency).

key_decisions.greek: ἔβαλεν τοὺς δακτύλους αὐτοῦ εἰς τὰ ὦτα αὐτοῦ
key_decisions.thai: ทรงเอานิ้วพระหัตถ์ยอนเข้าไปในหูของเขา
key_decisions.rationale: Per uW figs-explicit: Jesus put one finger into each ear (one from each hand). Royal 'นิ้วพระหัตถ์' (royal-finger-of-royal-hand) — preserving the divine-honorific even for body parts (RULES.md §3). 'ยอนเข้าไป' (insert/stick-into) for ἔβαλεν εἰς (literally 'cast into,' here = inserted). Thai 'หูของเขา' (his ear) — non-honorific for the ordinary man's ear. The asymmetry is deliberate: Jesus' royal body acts upon the ordinary human body.

key_decisions.thai: ทรงถ่มน้ำลายและแตะลิ้นของเขา
key_decisions.rationale: Per uW figs-extrainfo: Mark does not specify WHERE Jesus spat (on the ground, on his fingers, on the tongue). We preserve the ambiguity: 'ทรงถ่มน้ำลายและแตะลิ้น' (royal-spit and touch tongue) — coordinated by 'และ' (and) leaves the relationship open. Royal 'ทรงถ่ม' (royal-spit) — surprisingly using royal honorific even for spitting! This is theologically significant: Jesus' bodily fluids are not desecrating but vehicle of healing (parallel to Mark 8:23 — same technique on a blind man; John 9:6 — spit-mixed mud). In ancient medicine, saliva was thought to have healing properties. ἅπτω = touch (cf. Mark 1:41 leper; 5:27–28 hemorrhaging woman) — Jesus' deliberate physical contact even with ritually-suspect categories. Thai 'แตะ' (touch lightly) standard.

translation.notes: UNUSUAL HEALING TECHNIQUE: in most Markan healings, Jesus heals by word or touch alone. Here he uses an elaborate set of physical gestures (fingers in ears, spit, touch the tongue, look up, sigh, command) — atypical of his usual practice. Why? Several proposed reasons: (1) Communicating with a deaf man requires non-verbal signs — Jesus signals what he is about to heal; (2) the gestures are pedagogical (the man can see what Jesus does even though he cannot hear); (3) ancient medical practice associated saliva with healing; (4) Mark wants the reader to grasp the gritty-bodily concreteness of Jesus' healing (not abstract spiritual transaction). The privacy (κατ’ ἰδίαν) is also notable — perhaps to spare the man's dignity, perhaps to dampen messianic-political enthusiasm (the messianic-secret motif: see v.36).

greek: καὶ ἀναβλέψας εἰς τὸν οὐρανὸν ἐστέναξεν,καὶ λέγει αὐτῷ·Εφφαθα,ὅ ἐστιν Διανοίχθητι·
bsb_english: And looking up to heaven, He sighed deeply and said to him, "Ephphatha!" (which means, "Be opened!").

translation.thai: พระองค์ทรงแหงนพระพักตร์ดูฟ้าสวรรค์ ทรงถอนพระทัย และตรัสกับเขาว่า 'เอฟฟาธา' ซึ่งแปลว่า 'จงเปิดออก'

key_decisions.thai: ทรงแหงนพระพักตร์ดูฟ้าสวรรค์
key_decisions.rationale: Per uW translate-symaction: looking up to heaven was a common posture for prayer in Jewish/Greco-Roman culture (cf. Mark 6:41 the 5000 feeding; John 17:1; Ps 121:1). Royal 'ทรงแหงน' (royal-tilt-up) + 'พระพักตร์' (royal-face — RULES.md §3 honorific noun for face). 'ฟ้าสวรรค์' (sky-heaven) for οὐρανός — captures both the literal sky and the heavenly realm where God dwells. Standard Thai biblical idiom for upward-prayerful gaze.

key_decisions.thai: ทรงถอนพระทัย
key_decisions.rationale: Per uW figs-extrainfo: στενάζω = sigh, groan. The verb's significance is debated — could indicate (a) deep prayer, (b) compassion for the man's affliction, (c) lament over the brokenness of creation that requires this healing, (d) cost of the healing itself. We preserve the ambiguity. Thai 'ทรงถอนพระทัย' = royal 'ทรง' + 'ถอนพระทัย' (pull-royal-heart) — the established Thai biblical-royal idiom for 'sigh,' parallel to common 'ถอนหายใจ' (sigh) but in royal register suitable for divine subjects (RULES.md §3). Captures both the audible breath and the inward weight of feeling.

key_decisions.thai: 'เอฟฟาธา' ซึ่งแปลว่า 'จงเปิดออก'
key_decisions.rationale: HAPAX (only here in NT, transliterated Aramaic): Ἐφφαθά = Aramaic אֶתְפַּתַּח 'ethpatach' = imperative passive of פתח (open) = 'be opened.' Per uW translate-transliterate: Mark transliterates and translates — the same pattern as Mark 5:41 (ταλιθα κουμ) and 7:11 (κορβᾶν). Per RULES.md §5 Aramaic transliterations: we preserve the foreign-word effect by transliterating the Aramaic into Thai script — 'เอฟฟาธา' (Thai-script approximation of Aramaic 'eppatha' / Greek-spelled εφφαθα). The gloss 'จงเปิดออก' (must-open-out) renders διανοίχθητι precisely: 'จง' jussive imperative + 'เปิดออก' open-out (with the διά- prefix's 'thoroughly/right-through' force preserved by 'ออก'). διανοίγω is intensive of ἀνοίγω — 'be fully opened.' Per uW figs-yousingular: addressed to the man (sg.) — but importantly, what is being commanded to be 'opened'? The man's ears? His tongue? Or the man himself? Greek leaves it open. Thai 'จงเปิดออก' preserves the indeterminacy.

translation.notes: HAPAX Ἐφφαθά (Aramaic transliteration). One of three Aramaic transliterations in Mark (5:41 ταλιθα κουμ; 7:11 κορβᾶν — Aramaic/Hebrew; 7:34 here). Mark preserves the actual Aramaic word Jesus spoke, gestures of authentic memory. Theologically: ἐφφαθά is a one-word imperative that opens BOTH the man's ears AND tongue simultaneously (per v.35) — though grammatically passive imperative singular, it functions as a totalizing healing-command. Some early liturgies (cf. Roman Catholic baptism rite) preserve 'effeta' as a baptismal moment of opening the catechumen to faith. The looking-up + sighing + word-of-command is Jesus' characteristic 'public prayer-then-act' pattern (cf. John 11:41–43 Lazarus; Mark 6:41 feeding 5000).

greek: καὶ ἠνοίγησαν αὐτοῦ αἱ ἀκοαί,καὶ ἐλύθη ὁ δεσμὸς τῆς γλώσσης αὐτοῦ,καὶ ἐλάλει ὀρθῶς·
bsb_english: Immediately the man's ears were opened and his tongue was released, and he began to speak plainly.

translation.thai: ทันใดนั้นหูของเขาก็เปิดออก และสายลิ้นของเขาก็หลุดออก เขาก็พูดได้ชัดเจน

key_decisions.thai: หูของเขาก็เปิดออก
key_decisions.rationale: Per uW figs-activepassive: aorist passive — 'his hearing-faculties were opened.' ἀκοαί (plural) = the hearings/hearing-organs. Thai 'หู' (ear) for clarity. The passive carries divine-passive force (it was Jesus' word that opened them, but Greek leaves the agent unstated — God-acting-through-Jesus). Per uW figs-metaphor: the metaphor of being-blocked-and-opened is preserved in Thai 'เปิดออก' (open-out) — vivid and natural.

key_decisions.greek: ἐλύθη ὁ δεσμὸς τῆς γλώσσης αὐτοῦ
key_decisions.thai: สายลิ้นของเขาก็หลุดออก
key_decisions.rationale: Per uW figs-metaphor: the inability-to-speak imagined as a 'bond/cord/restraint' (δεσμός) on the tongue. When healed, the bond is 'loosed' (λύω, aor. pass.). Thai 'สายลิ้น' (cord-of-tongue, lit. 'tongue-string') is a natural Thai idiom — Thai actually has the colloquial 'ลิ้นพันกัน' (tongue-tied) for similar conditions. 'หลุดออก' (loose-out) for ἐλύθη preserves the unbinding image. The metaphor is medically apt: tongue-tied (ankyloglossia) is literally a restraining frenulum.

key_decisions.thai: เขาก็พูดได้ชัดเจน
key_decisions.rationale: Imperfect ἐλάλει = 'began to speak / kept speaking' (inceptive-durative). ὀρθῶς = correctly, plainly, clearly (not just 'at all' but 'normally'). Thai 'พูดได้ชัดเจน' (speak-can-clearly) renders the clarity-of-speech sense — important: the man was not merely making sounds but speaking properly, indicating full restoration. The Isaiah-35 echo continues: 'the tongue of the dumb shall sing for joy.'

translation.notes: ISA 35:5–6 FULFILLMENT: explicit. 'The eyes of the blind shall be opened, and the ears of the deaf shall be unstopped ... the tongue of the dumb (μογιλάλων) shall sing for joy.' Mark's vocabulary tracks Isaiah's promised Messianic-age physical-restoration. The instantaneous reversal — ears opened, tongue loosed, plain speech — is Mark's signature 'εὐθὺς' (immediately) pattern though the adverb is implicit here. The double passive (ἠνοίγησαν, ἐλύθη) is divine-passive: the agency is Jesus'-command-as-God's-power.

greek: καὶ διεστείλατο αὐτοῖς ἵνα μηδενὶ λέγωσιν·ὅσον δὲ αὐτοῖς διεστέλλετο,αὐτοὶ μᾶλλον περισσότερον ἐκήρυσσον.
bsb_english: Jesus ordered them not to tell anyone. But the more He ordered them, the more widely they proclaimed it.

translation.thai: พระองค์ทรงกำชับพวกเขาไม่ให้บอกผู้ใด แต่ยิ่งทรงกำชับเท่าใด พวกเขาก็ยิ่งประกาศเรื่องนี้แพร่หลายมากขึ้นเท่านั้น

key_decisions.thai: ทรงกำชับพวกเขาไม่ให้บอกผู้ใด
key_decisions.rationale: διαστέλλω = give strict order, command (intensive of στέλλω 'send'). Royal 'ทรงกำชับ' (royal-charge-strictly) for the imperative-strength. The MESSIANIC SECRET command (cf. Mark 1:43, 1:44, 3:12, 5:43, 8:30, 9:9) — recurrent Markan motif. Jesus repeatedly suppresses public messianic-acclaim, perhaps to delay political-messianic enthusiasm that would derail his suffering-servant trajectory.

key_decisions.greek: ὅσον ... διεστέλλετο, αὐτοὶ μᾶλλον περισσότερον ἐκήρυσσον (HAPAX περισσότερον in this comparative form)
key_decisions.thai: ยิ่งทรงกำชับเท่าใด ... ก็ยิ่งประกาศ ... แพร่หลายมากขึ้นเท่านั้น
key_decisions.rationale: ὅσον ... μᾶλλον = correlative comparative ('the more ... the more'). Per glossary-flag, περισσότερον is technically a comparative-adverb form of περισσός 'abundant' — but here it functions as 'more abundantly,' a hapax-class form (the regular adverb is περισσῶς; the comparative neuter accusative as adverb is rare). Thai 'ยิ่ง ... เท่าใด ... ก็ยิ่ง ... มากขึ้นเท่านั้น' is the standard Thai 'the more X, the more Y' construction — exactly the Greek correlative. 'แพร่หลาย' (spread-widely) for the publicity-spreading sense. The verbal effect: Jesus' silencing-command paradoxically intensifies the proclamation. The Markan irony: every silenced witness becomes a louder witness.

translation.notes: MESSIANIC SECRET pattern: the sixth and one of the most striking of Mark's silencing-commands. Why so insistent? William Wrede's classic theory: Mark constructs the messianic-secret to explain why Jesus' messianic identity was not widely recognized in his lifetime. More likely: Jesus suppresses miracle-fame because (a) it would trigger zealot-political messianism, derailing his suffering-servant mission; (b) the cross, not the miracles, must define his messianic identity (cf. Mark 8:30–31 — silence IMMEDIATELY followed by passion-prediction). HAPAX-CLASS: περισσότερον as adverb = 'more abundantly' — a rare comparative form, prepares the way for ὑπερπερισσῶς (super-abundantly) in v.37, both intensifying-superlatives. Mark loves these accumulating intensifiers.

greek: καὶ ὑπερπερισσῶς ἐξεπλήσσοντο λέγοντες·Καλῶς πάντα πεποίηκεν,καὶ τοὺς κωφοὺς ποιεῖ ἀκούειν καὶ ἀλάλους λαλεῖν.
bsb_english: The people were utterly astonished and said, "He has done all things well! He makes even the deaf hear and the mute speak!"

translation.thai: ผู้คนต่างอัศจรรย์ใจอย่างเหลือล้น และพูดกันว่า 'พระองค์ทรงกระทำทุกสิ่งได้ดียิ่งนัก พระองค์ทรงโปรดให้คนหูหนวกได้ยิน และคนใบ้พูดได้'

key_decisions.thai: อย่างเหลือล้น
key_decisions.rationale: HAPAX (only here in NT): ὑπερ-περισσῶς = super-abundantly, beyond-measure. Adverbial intensification using the ὑπερ- prefix that Paul loves (cf. Eph 3:20 ὑπερ-εκπερισσοῦ). The form stacks intensifiers (ὑπερ + περισσός) — Mark/the crowd's astonishment is so profound that even the standard 'περισσῶς' (exceedingly) requires further amplification. Thai 'อย่างเหลือล้น' (in-overflowing-manner) renders the over-the-top quantitative intensity. Combined with ἐξεπλήσσοντο ('were astonished,' established Markan ใช้ 'อัศจรรย์ใจ' Mark 6:2 etc.), the construction maximizes amazement.

key_decisions.thai: ทรงกระทำทุกสิ่งได้ดียิ่งนัก
key_decisions.rationale: Perfect tense πεποίηκεν = 'has done and the deeds stand' — abiding accomplishment. Per uW figs-explicit: 'all things' refers to all Jesus' deeds. CRUCIAL OT ECHO: Καλῶς ... πάντα + ποιέω deliberately echoes LXX Gen 1:31 'καὶ εἶδεν ὁ θεὸς τὰ πάντα ὅσα ἐποίησεν, καὶ ἰδοὺ καλὰ λίαν' ('and God saw all that he had made, and behold, it was very good'). The crowd's confession unwittingly attributes to Jesus the language of God-the-creator. Combined with the Isa 35:5–6 echo of v.32 (the same crowd notices specifically the deaf-hearing and mute-speaking, the exact Messianic signs of Isa 35), Mark frames Jesus' Gentile-territory ministry as both creational (Gen 1) and Messianic (Isa 35). Royal 'ทรงกระทำ' for Jesus' action; 'ดียิ่งนัก' (good-extremely) renders καλῶς (well/good) intensified.

key_decisions.greek: τοὺς κωφοὺς ποιεῖ ἀκούειν καὶ ἀλάλους λαλεῖν
key_decisions.thai: ทรงโปรดให้คนหูหนวกได้ยิน และคนใบ้พูดได้
key_decisions.rationale: Per uW figs-nominaladj: substantive use of adjectives — 'the deaf ones' and 'mute ones.' Thai 'คนหูหนวก' (ear-deaf person) for κωφούς; 'คนใบ้' (silent-person) for ἀλάλους — established Thai biblical idiom. Royal 'ทรงโปรดให้' (royal-grace-give-for) elegantly renders the causative ποιεῖ + infinitive 'makes them to do X' — 'โปรดให้' captures both the causative force and the divine-favor-giving aspect that the perfect-tense achievement implies. Note: ἄλαλος ('mute') here, NOT μογιλάλος ('hardly-speaking') from v.32 — a slight broadening, perhaps because the crowd's praise generalizes to many such healings, or because the man's halting-speech has fully resolved into normal-speech. Either way, the Isa 35:5–6 LXX echo is unmistakable: 'τότε ... ἀκούσονται κωφοί ... καὶ τρανὴ ἔσται γλῶσσα μογιλάλων.'

translation.notes: HAPAX ὑπερπερισσῶς. DOUBLE OT ECHO: (1) Gen 1:31 LXX 'πάντα ... καλά' — the crowd's 'καλῶς πάντα πεποίηκεν' attributes creational-good language to Jesus' deeds. (2) Isa 35:5–6 LXX — the deaf-hear / mute-speak combination is the explicit Messianic-restoration sign-set. Together: Mark presents Jesus' Gentile-territory ministry as the in-breaking of new-creation Messianic-restoration. The crowd is unwittingly orthodox. CHIASM: the chapter opens with Pharisees/scribes accusing the wrong defilement (vv.1–13) and closes with a Gentile crowd confessing the right Messianic-sign (v.37). Inside-out has been applied not just to ethics but to ethnics: the 'unclean' Gentiles confess what the 'clean' religious establishment refused to see. Add to nt_ot_citations.json.
